attributes.stem:
A 58-year-old man is brought to the Emergency Department by ambulance after being found confused and unable to walk properly outside a shopping centre. His friend reports he has a 20-year history of heavy alcohol use and has been drinking a bottle of spirits daily for the past two months. He has been eating only one meal every few days. He has no significant medical history and does not take any regular medications.

On examination, he appears malnourished and dishevelled. His pulse rate is 110/min, blood pressure 135/85 mmHg, respiratory rate 18/min, SpO2 98% on room air, and temperature 36.8°C. He is confused and disorientated to time and place. Neurological examination reveals bilateral lateral rectus palsy with restricted lateral eye movements, horizontal nystagmus, and an ataxic gait with inability to perform heel-to-toe walking. There is no evidence of trauma.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most appropriate immediate management?
attributes.explanation:
This patient presents with the classic triad of Wernicke's encephalopathy: confusion, ataxia, and ophthalmoplegia (specifically lateral rectus palsy). This neurological emergency occurs due to thiamine deficiency, commonly seen in chronic alcohol users with poor nutritional intake. The prolonged heavy alcohol use, severely restricted dietary intake, and malnourished appearance make this diagnosis highly likely.

Intravenous thiamine must be administered immediately. Wernicke's encephalopathy is a clinical diagnosis, and treatment should not be delayed for investigations. The recommended dose is 500mg three times daily for at least two to three days. Early treatment can prevent progression to Korsakoff syndrome, which causes permanent memory impairment.

Intravenous glucose should be avoided before thiamine administration in patients at risk of Wernicke's encephalopathy. Glucose metabolism requires thiamine as a cofactor, and giving glucose without thiamine can precipitate or worsen Wernicke's encephalopathy by depleting remaining thiamine stores.

Oral thiamine has poor bioavailability and is inadequate for treating established Wernicke's encephalopathy. Intravenous administration is required to achieve therapeutic levels rapidly.

Computed tomography brain scan may be performed to exclude other pathologies, but should not delay thiamine administration. CT findings in Wernicke's are often normal or non-specific. MRI is more sensitive but still should not delay treatment.

Blood glucose level measurement is reasonable as part of the workup, but hypoglycaemia typically presents with different neurological signs. The specific combination of ophthalmoplegia with ataxia and confusion in a malnourished patient with alcohol use disorder strongly suggests Wernicke's encephalopathy rather than hypoglycaemia.

It is important to note that the complete triad is present in only about 10% of cases. Emergency physicians regularly diagnose and initiate treatment for Wernicke's encephalopathy, as delayed treatment can result in irreversible neurological damage.
learningPoints: Wernicke's encephalopathy is characterised by confusion, ataxia, and ophthalmoplegia in the context of thiamine deficiency.
The complete triad is present in only 10% of cases, but any combination of these features in a patient at risk should prompt immediate thiamine replacement.
Thiamine must be given before glucose in patients at risk to prevent precipitating Wernicke's encephalopathy.
references: McLean C, Ivers R, Antony A, McMahon AT. Malnutrition, nutritional deficiency and alcohol: A guide for general practice. Australian journal of general practice. 2024 Apr;53(4):173-8.
Galvin R, Bråthen G, Ivashynka A, Hillbom M, Tanasescu R, Leone MA. EFNS guidelines for diagnosis, therapy and prevention of Wernicke encephalopathy. Eur J Neurol. 2010;17(12):1408-18.
